commonProblems.h2: Common Mercedes-Benz Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Mercedes-Benz diesel engines — particularly the OM651 2.1 CDI and OM642 3.0 V6 CDI — dominate UK replacement demand due to timing chain stretch, oil pressure loss, and DPF/EGR failures. Petrol M276 and M271 engines also suffer from camshaft adjuster and balance shaft issues. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below we break down the most common failures, what genuine repair costs look like at dealerships versus independent specialists, and when replacement actually makes sense.

problems.group: OM651 Timing Chain & Oil Pressure Failure
problems.h4: OM651 Timing Chain & Oil Pressure Failure – Why Mercedes 2.1 CDI Engines Snap Chains
problems.rootCause: The single-row timing chain stretches prematurely due to inadequate oil pressure at startup and a poorly designed chain tensioner. Extended oil change intervals exacerbate wear. When the chain jumps, valves contact pistons. Oil pressure loss also damages the variable valve timing solenoids and cam followers.
problems.recommendation: On a Mercedes C220 CDI worth £3,000–£6,000, preventative chain replacement at a specialist (before any rattling or fault codes) is the best value. Once the chain has jumped, the cylinder head repair cost approaches the price of a fully reconditioned engine with upgraded components — we recommend full replacement with a double-row chain unit. For Sprinter vans, replacement always makes sense due to downtime costs.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt OM651 engine prices for your C-Class, E-Class, Sprinter or Vito

problems.group: OM642 V6 Diesel Oil Cooler & Turbo Failure
problems.h4: OM642 V6 Diesel Oil Cooler & Turbo Failure – The 3.0 CDI Weak Spot
problems.rootCause: The plastic oil cooler housing located in the engine "V" degrades and leaks oil externally and internally. Oil leaks contaminate the turbocharger feed pipe seals, causing turbo oil starvation and bearing seizure. Simultaneously, the crankcase breather blocks, increasing pressure and forcing oil past seals.
problems.recommendation: If you catch the oil cooler leak early (visible oil dripping onto the undertray or bellhousing), the specialist minor repair at £700–£1,100 is excellent value. Once the turbo fails, you face a decision: on an E350 CDI worth £4,000–£7,000, a mid‑range turbo replacement buys time. But if the engine has run low on oil for any period, bottom end damage is likely — go straight to a recon OM642 with upgraded cooler and warranty.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for reconditioned OM642 engines with metal oil coolers

problems.h4: M272/M273 Balance Shaft & Timing Sprocket Failure – Mercedes Petrol V6/V8 Weakness
problems.rootCause: The balance shaft sprocket on early M272 and M273 engines had insufficient surface hardening. The sprocket teeth wear down, causing the camshaft timing to drift. The engine runs with a persistent check engine light, reduced power, rough idle, and eventual timing chain jump.
problems.recommendation: This failure gives warning — a persistent check engine light with codes P0016/P0017. On a C350 or E350 worth £2,500–£5,000, if caught immediately, the specialist balance sprocket replacement buys another 80,000+ miles. However, if the engine has been driven with the fault for months or has developed a rough idle and stalling, internal damage is present — replacement is the only safe bet.
problems.cta: → Compare reconditioned M272 and M273 engine prices for your petrol Mercedes

problems.h4: OM654 2.0 CDI AdBlue & Injector Seizure – Modern Diesel Failure
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue (DEF) system crystallises in the injector and SCR catalyst, leading to blocked injectors, NOx sensor failure, and forced engine derate. Separately, diesel injectors seize in the cylinder head due to inadequate anti‑seize compound from factory, making removal impossible without destroying the head.
problems.recommendation: The OM654 is otherwise a strong engine, but AdBlue failures are common on 2016–2019 models — a specialist minor repair at £500–£850 is cost‑effective. For seized injectors, we recommend replacement only if an injector removal specialist confirms bores are salvageable. On a 2017 E220d worth £8,000–£12,000, a recon engine with warranty (£3,400–£5,000) is safer than throwing £1,800 at a head repair that may fail within months.
problems.cta: → Compare reconditioned OM654 engine prices for your E220d or C220d

problems.h4: Crankshaft & Bearing Failure – OM642 and OM651 Oil Starvation Catastrophe
problems.rootCause: Oil pickup strainer becomes partially blocked by timing chain guide debris and sludge from extended oil change intervals. Oil pressure drops below 1 bar at hot idle — insufficient for crankshaft bearings. The result is spun bearings, crankshaft journal scoring, and rod knock that appears without any prior engine warning light.
problems.recommendation: There is no safe preventative tier for crankshaft bearing failure on OM651 or OM642 — once rod knock is audible, damage is already severe. Regrinding the crankshaft is possible but carries a 30–40% risk of early repeat failure due to the high torsional loads of these diesel engines. On a Sprinter worth £4,000–£8,000, we recommend full replacement with a reconditioned engine and warranty. The extra £900–£1,600 buys certainty.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for fully reconditioned OM651 or OM642 engines with new crankshafts

engineSizes.h2: Mercedes-Benz Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Mercedes-Benz engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0‑litre diesel is Mercedes‑Benz’s highest‑volume engine across C‑Class, E‑Class, Sprinter, and Vito vans. The OM651 (2008‑2016) dominates earlier models with its problematic single‑row timing chain, while the newer OM654 (2016‑present) brought stepped recess pistons and greater efficiency but suffers from AdBlue crystallisation and injector seizure. This displacement accounts for the majority of Mercedes diesel replacement enquiries in the UK due to chain stretch and oil pressure loss on OM651, and AdBlue system failures on OM654. Engine code(s): OM651, OM654 Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W204/W205), E‑Class (W212/W213), Sprinter (906/907), Vito (W639/W447), GLC (X253), S‑Class (W222 – OM654 late models) Production years: 2008–present Common failure points: OM651 – timing chain stretch, oil pressure loss, crankshaft bearing failure; OM654 – AdBlue injector crystallisation, seized injectors in cylinder head
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 2.1L Diesel Engines
items.description: Although very close in displacement to the 2.0L, the 2.1‑litre OM646 (2002‑2010) was the predecessor to the OM651 in many C‑Class, E‑Class, and Sprinter models. This engine uses a timing chain that is more reliable than the OM651 but suffers from injector seal leaks (black death), EGR valve clogging, and crankshaft pulley failure. Replacement demand on OM646 is lower than OM651 but still significant, particularly for high‑mileage fleet vehicles where injector seal failure has led to cylinder head damage. Engine code(s): OM646 (646.811, 646.821, 646.961 – various) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W203), E‑Class (W211), Sprinter (906 early), Vito (W639) Production years: 2002–2010 Common failure points: Injector seal leakage ("black death"), EGR valve clogging, crankshaft pulley damper failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz 2.1L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 2.7L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.7‑litre OM612 and OM647 straight‑five diesels are now rare but still sought after for Mercedes Sprinter (T1N) and early E‑Class (W210/W211) owners. These engines are generally durable with fewer catastrophic failures than later common‑rail units, but injector seal leaks (black death) and high‑pressure fuel pump failure do occur. Replacement demand is low but persistent from commercial fleet owners and classic preservationists seeking direct‑fit recon units. Engine code(s): OM612 (612.981), OM647 (647.981) Compatible models (UK): Sprinter (T1N, 1995‑2006), E‑Class (W210/W211), C‑Class (W203 – rare) Production years: 1999–2006 Common failure points: Injector seal leakage, high‑pressure fuel pump failure, injector return line leaks
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz 2.7L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0‑litre V6 diesel (OM642) is Mercedes’ most common larger diesel, fitted across E‑Class, ML‑Class, GL‑Class, Sprinter, and S‑Class. It is powerful and refined but suffers from three expensive failure modes: plastic oil cooler housing leaking oil (often destroying the turbo), crankcase breather blockage, and crankshaft bearing failure due to oil strainer blockage. Replacement demand is high — particularly on ML350 and E350 models where oil cooler leakage has been ignored until turbo seizure or bottom end knock develops. Engine code(s): OM642 (642.820, 642.850, 642.920 – numerous variants) Compatible models (UK): E‑Class (W211/W212/W213), ML‑Class (W164/W166), GL‑Class (X164), Sprinter (906/907), S‑Class (W221/W222), GLE (W166), C‑Class (W204 – C350 CDI) Production years: 2005–present Common failure points: Plastic oil cooler housing leaks, turbocharger oil starvation, crankcase breather blockage, crankshaft bearing failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 4.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 4.0‑litre OM629 V8 diesel was a low‑production engine reserved for top‑tier S‑Class (W221) and GL‑Class (X164) models. It produces substantial torque but suffers from carbon buildup in the intake manifold, EGR cooler failure, and crankshaft bearing wear at high mileages. Replacement demand is very low due to limited UK sales; most owners who experience major failure choose vehicle scrappage rather than engine replacement due to recon unit scarcity and high cost (£7,000–£10,000 specialist supply & fit). Engine code(s): OM629 (629.910, 629.911) Compatible models (UK): S‑Class (W221 – S420 CDI), GL‑Class (X164 – GL420 CDI) Production years: 2006–2010 Common failure points: Intake carbon buildup, EGR cooler failure, crankshaft bearing wear
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz 4.0L diesel engine availability

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6‑litre M270 turbocharged four‑cylinder petrol engine was introduced with the A‑Class and B‑Class (W176/W246) as part of Mercedes’ front‑wheel‑drive compact strategy. It is generally reliable but suffers from high‑pressure fuel pump failure, thermostat housing leaks, and carbon buildup on intake valves due to direct injection. Replacement demand is low compared to diesel but present in high‑mileage A180 and B180 models where overheating from coolant loss has caused head gasket failure. Engine code(s): M270 (270.910, 270.911 – 1.6L variants) Compatible models (UK): A‑Class (W176 A180), B‑Class (W246 B180), CLA (C117 CLA180), GLA (X156 GLA180) Production years: 2011–2018 Common failure points: High‑pressure fuel pump failure, carbon buildup on intake valves, thermostat housing leaks
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8‑litre supercharged M271 engine (often badged "Kompressor") was fitted to early C‑Class (W203), E‑Class (W211), and SLK (R171). It suffers from a design flaw in the camshaft adjuster (gears wear and cause timing drift) and balance shaft sprocket wear leading to catastrophic valve‑piston contact. Replacement demand is moderate from owners of sub‑£3,000 C180 and C200 Kompressor models where timing failure has written off the engine. Engine code(s): M271 (271.940, 271.946, 271.950 – 1.8L supercharged variants) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W203 C180/C200 Kompressor), E‑Class (W211 E200 Kompressor), SLK (R171 SLK200) Production years: 2002–2009 Common failure points: Camshaft adjuster gear wear, balance shaft sprocket wear, timing chain stretch
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0‑litre turbocharged M274 (2013‑2018) and M260 (2018‑present) petrol engines replaced the older M271 across C‑Class, E‑Class, GLC, and A‑Class models. M274 engines are reliable overall but suffer from high‑pressure fuel pump failure, camshaft adjuster solenoid leaks, and thermostat housing cracks. Replacement demand is low but growing as earlier M274 units now exceed 100,000 miles. The performance variant (M133 in A45 AMG) has significantly higher failure rates with turbo seal failure and piston ring wear. Engine code(s): M274 (274.920 – 2.0L turbo), M260 (260.920 – 2.0L turbo), M133 (133.980 – A45 AMG) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W205 C200/C250), E‑Class (W213 E200), GLC (X253 GLC200), A‑Class (W177 A200/A250/A45 AMG), CLA (C118), GLA (H247) Production years: 2013–present Common failure points: M274 – high‑pressure fuel pump, thermostat housing, camshaft adjuster solenoids; M133 – turbo seal failure, piston ring wear (high‑output variants)
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5‑litre M272 V6 petrol engine (badged 2.5 or 250) was a short‑lived displacement offered mostly outside the UK but appears in some W204 C250 and early W212 E250 models. It shares the same catastrophic weakness as larger M272/M273 engines: the balance shaft sprocket wears prematurely, causing cam timing drift, check engine lights (P0016/P0017), and eventual timing chain jump. Replacement demand in the UK is very low due to limited sales, but specialist recon units are still available. Engine code(s): M272 (272.920 – 2.5L V6) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W204 C250), E‑Class (W212 E250 – early, rare) Production years: 2005–2009 Common failure points: Balance shaft sprocket wear (hardening failure), camshaft timing drift, timing chain jump
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz 2.5L petrol engine availability

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.0‑litre M272 and M276 V6 petrol engines were widely fitted to C‑Class, E‑Class, SLK, and ML‑Class models as the "300" or "350" (detuned variants). The M272 (pre‑2010) suffers from the same balance shaft sprocket wear as the 2.5L, while the M276 (2010‑2018) is more reliable but has high‑pressure fuel pump and camshaft adjuster issues. Replacement demand is moderate, particularly for M272 units where timing failure has destroyed the engine. Specialist rebuilt M276 engines are also requested due to oil cooler leaks and cylinder scoring. Engine code(s): M272 (272.940, 272.945 – 3.0L V6), M276 (276.950, 276.952 – 3.0L V6) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W204 C300), E‑Class (W212 E300), SLK (R171/R172 SLK300), ML‑Class (W164 ML300) Production years: 2005–2018 Common failure points: M272 – balance shaft sprocket, camshaft adjuster wear; M276 – high‑pressure fuel pump, camshaft adjuster solenoids, oil cooler leaks
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 3.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.5‑litre M272 (early) and M276 (late) V6 petrol engines were Mercedes’ high‑volume "350" badged engines across the range. The M272 variant shares the balance shaft sprocket failure — a known catastrophic weakness that often writes off sub‑£5,000 C350, E350, and ML350 examples. The later M276 (direct‑injection) has fewer catastrophic failures but suffers from internal timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster wear. Replacement demand is significant for pre‑2010 models with balance shaft failure, and moderate for M276 units with chain stretch. Engine code(s): M272 (272.960, 272.965 – 3.5L V6), M276 (276.960 – 3.5L V6) Compatible models (UK): C‑Class (W204 C350), E‑Class (W211/W212 E350), ML‑Class (W164 ML350), SLK (R171/R172 SLK350), S‑Class (W221 S350 – petrol) Production years: 2005–2016 Common failure points: M272 – balance shaft sprocket wear (pre‑2008), camshaft adjuster wear; M276 – timing chain stretch, camshaft adjuster solenoids
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz 3.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 4.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.0‑litre M176 and M177 twin‑turbo V8 petrol engines power AMG models (C63, E63, GLC63) and high‑end GLE/GLS models. They are precision‑engineered performance units but suffer from turbocharger oil line leaks, cylinder scoring in high‑output variants, and high‑pressure fuel pump failures. Replacement demand is low but extremely high‑value (£8,000–£15,000 for a recon unit). Most owners of C63 or E63 with bottom end failure or bore scoring opt for specialist rebuilt engines with upgraded turbo oil feeds. Engine code(s): M176 (176.980), M177 (177.980 – 4.0L twin‑turbo V8), M178 (178.980 – AMG GT – similar family) Compatible models (UK): C63 (W205), E63 (W213), GLC63 (X253), GLE63 (W166), GLS63 (X166), S63 (W222), AMG GT Production years: 2015–present Common failure points: Turbocharger oil line leaks, cylinder scoring (high‑output variants), high‑pressure fuel pump failure, oil seepage from cam covers
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz 4.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 5.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.0‑litre M113 V8 petrol engine (pre‑2006) and M119 V8 (rare in UK) are now classic‑era units found in R230 SL500, W220 S500, and CL500 models. These engines are generally durable with few catastrophic failures, but valve stem seal wear (causing blue smoke on startup), timing chain guide embrittlement, and intake manifold swirl flaps sticking are common at high mileages. Replacement demand is very low; owners of these vehicles typically choose rebuilds of their existing engine rather than recon exchange units due to the classic car market. Engine code(s): M113 (113.960 – 5.0L V8), M119 (119.980 – earlier, very rare) Compatible models (UK): SL (R230 SL500), S‑Class (W220 S500), CL (C215 CL500), E‑Class (W211 E500 – early) Production years: 1998–2006 Common failure points: Valve stem seal wear (oil burning), timing chain guide embrittlement, intake manifold swirl flap failure
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz 5.0L petrol engine availability

items.title: Mercedes-Benz 5.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.5‑litre M273 V8 (naturally aspirated) and M157 twin‑turbo V8 engines represent Mercedes’ high‑power petrol offerings in the late 2000s and 2010s. The M273 (2006‑2011) suffers from the same balance shaft sprocket wear as the M272 V6 — a catastrophic failure requiring engine replacement. The M157 (2011‑2017) is more robust but has timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster solenoid failures. Replacement demand is low but expensive (£6,000–£12,000), mostly for M273 units with balance shaft failure in E500 and S500 models. Engine code(s): M273 (273.960 – 5.5L V8), M157 (157.980 – 5.5L twin‑turbo V8) Compatible models (UK): E‑Class (W211/W212 E500), S‑Class (W221 S500), CL (C216 CL500), ML (W164 ML500), E63 AMG (pre‑2014 – M157 variant), S63 AMG (W221/W222 – M157) Production years: 2006–2017 Common failure points: M273 – balance shaft sprocket wear; M157 – timing chain stretch, camshaft adjuster solenoids, high‑pressure fuel pump Closing line: Not sure which engine size your Mercedes-Benz needs? items.title: Mercedes-Benz Diesel Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Mercedes-Benz diesel engines account for over 75% of UK replacement enquiries, with the OM651 2.1 CDI in C‑Class, E‑Class, Sprinter and Vito dominating the market, followed by the OM642 3.0 V6 CDI in larger SUVs and premium saloons. Common Mercedes-Benz Diesel Engine Families: OM646 – 2.1L straight‑four (2002‑2010), predecessor to OM651, injector seal "black death" common OM651 – 2.1L straight‑four (2008‑2016), most replaced Mercedes diesel, timing chain and oil pressure failures OM654 – 2.0L straight‑four (2016‑present), modern replacement for OM651, AdBlue crystallisation and injector seizure OM642 – 3.0L V6 (2005‑present), oil cooler housing leaks destroying turbochargers, crankshaft bearing failure OM629 – 4.0L V8 (2006‑2010), rare S420 CDI and GL420 CDI, carbon buildup and EGR cooler failure Found In: C‑Class (W203/W204/W205) – OM646, OM651, OM654 (late W205), OM642 (C350 CDI) E‑Class (W211/W212/W213) – OM646 (early W211), OM651, OM654, OM642 (E350 CDI) S‑Class (W221/W222) – OM642 (S350 CDI), OM629 (S420 CDI) Sprinter (T1N/906/907) – OM611, OM646, OM651, OM642 (3.0L V6 in 906/907) Vito (W639/W447) – OM646, OM651, OM654 (W447) ML/GLE/GLC (W164/W166/X253) – OM642, OM651 (GLC220d), OM654 (GLC late) GL/GLS (X164/X166) – OM642, OM629 (GL420 CDI) Known For (Brand‑wide): Exceptional motorway fuel economy – 50‑60mpg from OM651 and OM654 in C/E‑Class OM651 single‑row timing chain stretch at 70‑120k miles – often writes off sub‑£4k cars OM642 plastic oil cooler housing leaks – turbo failure follows if ignored OM654 AdBlue injector crystallisation – forced engine derate with countdown High replacement demand from Sprinter/Vito fleet operators – downtime is expensive Crankshaft bearing failure on both OM651 and OM642 due to oil strainer blockage Typical Diesel Models (UK): C220 CDI (W204, OM651) E220 CDI / E220d (W212/W213, OM651/OM654) E350 CDI (W212, OM642) Sprinter 2.1 CDI (906, OM651) ML350 CDI (W164/W166, OM642) GLC220d (X253, OM654)

items.title: Mercedes-Benz Petrol Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Mercedes-Benz petrol engines cover everything from supercharged 1.8L Kompressor units in early C‑Class to twin‑turbo 4.0L V8s in AMG models – with M272/M273 balance shaft failures driving replacement demand on 2005‑2010 V6/V8 engines. Common Mercedes-Benz Petrol Engine Families: M271 – 1.8L supercharged straight‑four (2002‑2009), camshaft adjuster and balance shaft wear M270 – 1.6L turbo straight‑four (2011‑2018), A/CLA/GLA Class, high‑pressure fuel pump failure M274 – 2.0L turbo straight‑four (2013‑2018), C/E/GLC Class, thermostat housing and camshaft solenoid leaks M260 – 2.0L turbo straight‑four (2018‑present), A/CLA/GLA Class, generally reliable M272 – 2.5L / 3.0L / 3.5L V6 (2005‑2011), balance shaft sprocket wear – catastrophic timing failure M276 – 3.0L / 3.5L V6 (2010‑2018), timing chain stretch across bank 1/2, camshaft adjuster issues M113 – 5.0L V8 (1998‑2006), SL500/S500/CL500, valve stem seal wear M273 – 5.5L V8 (2006‑2011), balance shaft sprocket wear (same weakness as M272) M157 – 5.5L twin‑turbo V8 (2011‑2017), E63/S63, timing chain stretch, high‑pressure fuel pump failure M176/M177 – 4.0L twin‑turbo V8 (2015‑present), C63/E63/GLC63, turbo oil line leaks, cylinder scoring Found In: A‑Class (W176/W177) – M270 (A180/A200), M260 (A200/A250), M133 (A45 AMG) C‑Class (W203/W204/W205) – M271, M272 (C280/C350), M274 (C200/C250/C300), M177 (C63) E‑Class (W211/W212/W213) – M271 (E200 Kompressor), M272 (E280/E350), M274 (E200/E300), M276 (E350/E400), M157 (E63), M177 (E63) S‑Class (W220/W221/W222) – M113 (S500), M273 (S500), M276 (S400), M157 (S63), M177 (S63) SL/SLK (R171/R172/R230) – M271 (SLK200), M272 (SLK280/SL350), M113 (SL500), M276 (SL400) ML/GLE/GLS (W164/W166) – M272 (ML350), M273 (ML500), M157 (ML63/GLE63) AMG GT – M178 (4.0L twin‑turbo V8, close relative of M177) Known For (Brand‑wide): M272/M273 balance shaft sprocket failure (2005‑2008 production) – writes off engines without warning M271 camshaft adjuster wear and timing chain stretch – common on supercharged 1.8L M276 internal timing chain stretch requiring engine‑out replacement on many variants M157 dual turbocharger system – oil line leaks and high fuel pressure system failures Petrol replacement demand is significantly lower than diesel – but AMG models drive high‑value enquiries Direct injection on M270/M274/M276 causes carbon buildup on intake valves Typical Petrol Models (UK): C180 / C200 Kompressor items.title: Mercedes-Benz Plug‑in Hybrid (PHEV) Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Mercedes-Benz PHEVs combine a petrol engine (typically M274 or M264 2.0L turbo) with an electric motor and battery, offering 20‑35 miles of electric‑only range – replacement demand is rare but specialist knowledge is required. Common Mercedes-Benz PHEV Engine Families: M274 – 2.0L turbo petrol (2016‑2020), used in C350e, E350e, GLC350e M264 – 2.0L turbo petrol (2018‑present), used in E300e, C300e, S580e How Mercedes-Benz PHEV Systems Work (brand‑wide): Electric motor integrated into 9G‑TRONIC automatic gearbox (or 7G‑TRONIC on early models) 6.1‑13.5 kWh lithium‑ion battery under rear seat or boot floor Regenerative braking recharges battery; external charging via Type 2 port Found In: C350e (W205) – M274 2.0L turbo + 60kW electric motor, 2015‑2018 E350e (W213) – M274 2.0L turbo + electric motor, 2016‑2020 E300e (W213) – M264 2.0L turbo + electric motor, 2019‑2023 C300e (W206) – M264 2.0L turbo + electric motor, 2021‑present GLC350e (X253) – M274 2.0L turbo + electric motor, 2017‑2020 S580e (W223) – M264 3.0L straight‑six + electric motor, 2021‑present Important Notes (Brand‑wide): The petrol engine block is identical to non‑hybrid M274/M264 – replacement engine is standard unit Electric motor sits inside gearbox – motor failure requires transmission specialist intervention Battery health inspection is critical when replacing any component in the hybrid system PHEV engine replacement is rare (under 5% of Mercedes enquiries) – most hybrid failures are battery or control electronics High voltage system isolation required before any engine work – only specialist workshops can perform

items.title: Mercedes-Benz Electric Powertrains
items.description: Short descriptor: Mercedes-Benz fully electric models (EQ brand) use eATS electric drivetrains with one or two motors – motor failure is extremely rare, but we connect you with UK HV battery and motor repair specialists. Common Mercedes-Benz Electric Motors: eATS 1.0 – 150‑250kW asynchronous motor, EQC (N293) eATS 2.0 – 180‑385kW permanently excited synchronous motor, EQS (V297), EQE (V295), EQA (H243), EQB (X243) How Mercedes-Benz Electric Powertrains Work: Single motor (rear‑wheel drive) or dual motor (4MATIC all‑wheel drive) on EQ models 70‑120 kWh lithium‑ion battery pack under floor, typical range 250‑400 miles Motor failure is extremely rare (<1% of powertrain faults) – battery/module repair or contactor failure is more common Found In: EQC (N293) – eATS 1.0 dual motor (300kW), 2019‑2023 EQS (V297) – eATS 2.0 single/dual motor (245‑385kW), 2021‑present EQE (V295) – eATS 2.0 single/dual motor (215‑350kW), 2022‑present EQA (H243) – eATS 2.0 single/dual motor (140‑250kW), 2021‑present EQB (X243) – eATS 2.0 single/dual motor (168‑250kW), 2022‑present EQV (W447) – eATS based on Vito EV (150kW), 2020‑present Important Notes: No traditional "engine" to replace – enquiries for "electric motor replacement" are exceptionally rare Labour rates for HV system work are higher than combustion engine repairs (specialist training and isolation equipment required) Battery degradation (capacity loss below 70%) is the most common high‑value repair, not motor failure EngineMarket connects you with approved Mercedes EQ specialists for battery repair, module replacement, and motor inspection Always request a full HV system health report before committing to any major repair or replacement

engineYears.h2: Mercedes-Benz Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.description: Short intro: The final years of Mercedes-Benz's pre-common-rail era, with the transition to CDI diesel technology beginning in 1999 and reshaping the brand's engine lineup permanently. Key Changes OM604/OM605/OM606 indirect injection diesels still fitted to outgoing E-Class and S-Class variants CDI common-rail diesel technology introduced in 1999, beginning with OM611 in the C-Class and E-Class M111 four-cylinder petrol family in active production across C-Class and SLK M112 V6 petrol introduced for mid-range E-Class and CLK models M113 V8 naturallyaspirated petrol unit debuts across S-Class and CL-Class Main Engines OM611 CDI - 2.1 diesel (C220 CDI, E220 CDI) 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction OM606 - 3.0 straight-six diesel (E300 Turbodiesel - outgoing) M111 - 1.8/2.0 four-cylinder petrol (C180, C200, SLK200) M112 - 2.4/2.6/3.2 V6 petrol (E240, E320, CLK320) M113 - 4.3/5.0 V8 petrol (S430, S500, CL500) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W202/W203) Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W210) Mercedes-Benz S-Class (W220) Mercedes-Benz CLK (W208) Mercedes-Benz SLK (R170) Known For OM606 straight-six diesel regarded as near-indestructible at high mileage - one of the most reliable diesels Mercedes-Benz ever produced Early OM611 CDI units offer significantly improved economy and refinement but early injector seals can weep above 100,000 miles M112 V6 petrol is a strong unit overall; balance shaft tensioner wear is a known issue on higher-mileage examples W210 E-Class bodywork notorious for accelerated rust, but engines themselves are mechanically well-regarded [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM611, OM606, OM605, OM604, M111, M112, M113
years.cta: Search for 1998–2000 Mercedes-Benz replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: CDI Expansion & OM646/OM647 Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Mercedes-Benz extended CDI technology across the full model range, introducing the new OM646 and OM647 engine families and retiring the last of the pre-common-rail units. Key Changes OM646 2.1-litre four-cylinder CDI replaces OM611 across C-Class and E-Class from 2002 OM647 2.7-litre five-cylinder CDI introduced for E270 CDI and Sprinter van derivatives OM648 3.2-litre six-cylinder CDI debuts in E320 CDI and S320 CDI, offering class-leading diesel refinement M271 supercharged four-cylinder petrol replaces M111 across C-Class and SLK from 2002 W203 C-Class and W211 E-Class platforms arrive, reshaping engine fitment across the range Main Engines OM646 - 2.1 four-cylinder CDI (C200 CDI, C220 CDI, E200 CDI, E220 CDI) OM647 - 2.7 five-cylinder CDI (E270 CDI, Sprinter 270) OM648 - 3.2 six-cylinder CDI (E320 CDI, S320 CDI, ML320 CDI) M271 - 1.8 supercharged four-cylinder petrol (C180 K, C200 K, SLK200 K) M112 / M113 - 2.6–5.0 V6 and V8 petrol (continued across E-Class, S-Class, SL) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W203) Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W211) Mercedes-Benz S-Class (W220) Mercedes-Benz ML-Class (W163/W164) Mercedes-Benz Vito/Sprinter (commercial) Known For OM646 is a durable workhorse in everyday use; EGR valve fouling is common from 80,000 miles and swirl flap failure is a known fault on high-mileage units OM648 3.2 CDI praised for smoothness and longevity; injector return pipe deterioration is the most common fault M271 supercharged units suffer timing chain wear - chain and tensioner replacement is often required between 80,000–120,000 miles W211 E-Class regarded as one of the finest driving Mercedes of the era; engine options largely reliable but electrical gremlins add cost [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM646, OM647, OM648, M271, M112, M113, OM611
years.cta: Compare prices for 2001–2004 Mercedes-Benz CDI replacement engines from UK rebuilders

years.preview: AMG V8 Expansion, OM642 V6 CDI & Euro 4 Compliance 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The OM642 3.0 V6 CDI arrived as Mercedes-Benz's most significant diesel architecture of the decade - a unit that would power the brand across cars, vans, and SUVs for fifteen years. Key Changes OM642 3.0-litre V6 CDI introduced across E320 CDI, ML320 CDI, R320 CDI, Sprinter - replacing the OM648 inline-six OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder CDI development begins (full launch 2008–09); pre-production models evaluated M156 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 AMG engine debuts in C63 AMG (W204) and E63 AMG Euro 4 emissions compliance across all diesel variants 7G-Tronic automatic gearbox becomes standard across most diesel and V6 petrol models Main Engines OM642 - 3.0 V6 CDI (E320 CDI, ML320 CDI, R320 CDI, GL320 CDI, Sprinter 3.0) OM646 - 2.1 four-cylinder CDI (C220 CDI, continued W203/W204 early production) M156 - 6.2 naturally aspirated V8 (C63 AMG, E63 AMG, SL63 AMG) M272 - 2.5/3.0/3.5 V6 petrol (C230, E350, CLK350, ML350) M273 - 4.7/5.5 V8 petrol (S450, S500, CL500) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W211) Mercedes-Benz ML-Class (W164) Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W204) Mercedes-Benz GL-Class (X164) Mercedes-Benz Sprinter (906) Known For OM642 V6 CDI is exceptionally smooth and powerful but prone to swirl flap failure - broken flap fragments can cause catastrophic engine damage if not caught early OM642 oil cooler seal failure is a well-documented fault, leading to coolant contamination of the oil circuit above 100,000 miles M156 6.2 AMG unit celebrated for raw, naturally aspirated character - intake cam adjuster wear is a known issue requiring specialist attention M272/M273 V6 and V8 petrol units are generally reliable; balance shaft gear wear on M272 between 100,000–150,000 miles is a documented fault [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM642, OM646, M156, M272, M273, M271, OM648
years.cta: Find replacement OM642 and M156 engines for 2005–2008 Mercedes-Benz models

years.preview: OM651 Modular Diesel, BlueTEC & Euro 5 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The OM651 2.1-litre CDI became the backbone of Mercedes-Benz's diesel range, replacing the OM646 across nearly every passenger car line and introducing BlueTEC emissions technology to UK buyers. Key Changes OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder CDI replaces OM646 across C-Class, E-Class, GLK, Sprinter, and Vito BlueTEC SCR (urea injection / AdBlue) technology introduced on OM642 V6 CDI models for Euro 5 compliance M274 2.0-litre turbocharged petrol engine development begins; early fitment from 2011 in SLK M278 4.7-litre biturbo V8 replaces naturally aspirated M273 in S-Class and CLS from 2010 7G-Tronic Plus replaces original 7G-Tronic on most new platforms Main Engines OM651 - 2.1 four-cylinder CDI (C200 CDI, C220 CDI, E200 CDI, E220 CDI, GLK220 CDI, Vito, Sprinter) OM642 BlueTEC - 3.0 V6 CDI (E350 BlueTEC, ML350 BlueTEC, GL350 BlueTEC) M278 - 4.7 biturbo V8 petrol (S500, CLS500, E500) M157 - 5.5 biturbo V8 AMG (E63 AMG, S63 AMG, CLS63 AMG) M274 - 2.0 turbo petrol (SLK250, early E200 petrol) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W204) Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W212) Mercedes-Benz GLK-Class (X204) Mercedes-Benz Sprinter (906) Mercedes-Benz Vito (W639) Known For OM651 is fuel-efficient and widespread but diesel particulate filter (DPF) blockage is extremely common on UK short-journey driving patterns OM651 timing chain tensioner wear is a documented fault - chain rattle on cold start is a well-known early warning sign M157 5.5 biturbo AMG engine delivers exceptional performance but camshaft adjuster wear and valve stem seal deterioration are recorded above 60,000 miles BlueTEC AdBlue dosing systems introduce an additional maintenance and failure point - NOx sensor and AdBlue pump failures are common from 80,000 miles [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM651, OM642, M274, M278, M157, M276
years.cta: Get quotes for 2008–2012 Mercedes-Benz OM651 and OM642 replacement engines

years.preview: Euro 6 Preparation, M274 Petrol Rollout & AMG GT Engine Debut 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Mercedes-Benz prepared its entire engine range for Euro 6 homologation while rolling out the M274 turbocharged petrol family across the C and E-Class, beginning the phaseout of the M271 supercharged unit. Key Changes M274 2.0-litre turbocharged petrol replaces M271 supercharged unit across C-Class, E-Class, and GLC Euro 6 diesel compliance achieved via revised OM651 and OM642 with improved SCR/AdBlue systems M177/M178 4.0-litre biturbo V8 AMG engine begins development; debuts in AMG GT (2014) 9G-Tronic nine-speed automatic gearbox introduced from 2013 on E-Class and S-Class New W205 C-Class and W222 S-Class platforms bring revised engine bay architecture Main Engines M274 - 2.0 turbo petrol (C200, C250, E200, E250, GLC250) OM651 Euro 6 - 2.1 CDI (C220d, E220d, GLC220d, Vito, Sprinter) OM642 BlueTEC Euro 6 - 3.0 V6 CDI (E350d, ML350d, GL350d) M178 - 4.0 biturbo V8 (AMG GT, AMG GT S - from 2014) M276 - 3.0 V6 turbo petrol (E400, S400, GLE400) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W205) Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W212 facelift / W213 early) Mercedes-Benz S-Class (W222) Mercedes-Benz GLC-Class (X253) Mercedes-Benz AMG GT (C190) Known For M274 delivers strong performance and economy but water pump failure is a known fault - internal coolant leaks into the oil circuit have been reported between 60,000–100,000 miles Euro 6 OM651 remains the most common diesel replacement enquiry in the UK market due to continued DPF and timing chain issues M178 4.0 biturbo V8 is one of the finest AMG engines ever produced - highly regarded for tunability and longevity when serviced correctly 9G-Tronic gearbox on early fitment models subject to software and mechatronic adaptation issues - not an engine fault but affects perceived engine performance [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: M274, OM651, OM642, M178, M276, M277
years.cta: Compare 2013–2015 Mercedes-Benz replacement engine quotes from verified UK suppliers

years.preview: OM654 Modular Diesel Launch & EQ Power Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The all-new OM654 2.0-litre diesel replaced the long-running OM651 as Mercedes-Benz's primary diesel architecture - a steel-piston, aluminium-block unit designed from the ground up to meet Euro 6c and beyond. Key Changes OM654 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel replaces OM651 from 2016 in E-Class (W213), followed by C-Class and GLC OM654 features steel pistons in aluminium block, twin-scroll turbo, and integrated exhaust manifold - a clean-sheet design EQ Power plug-in hybrid technology introduced (C350e, E350e) combining M274 petrol with electric motor OM642 V6 diesel begins phaseout on passenger cars - retained in G-Class and commercial range 48V MHEV (mild hybrid) electrical architecture introduced on S-Class from 2017 Main Engines OM654 - 2.0 four-cylinder diesel (E220d, C220d, GLC220d, A220d) M274 + electric - PHEV 2.0 turbo petrol hybrid (C350e, E350e) OM642 - 3.0 V6 diesel (G350d, retained Sprinter variants) M178 - 4.0 biturbo V8 (AMG C63, AMG E63, AMG GLC63) M264 - 1.5/2.0 four-cylinder turbo petrol (A-Class, CLA - from 2018) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W213) Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W205 facelift) Mercedes-Benz GLC-Class (X253) Mercedes-Benz A-Class (W177 - from 2018) Mercedes-Benz C 63 AMG / E 63 AMG Known For OM654 is widely regarded as a significant improvement over OM651 in refinement and emissions compliance; early examples show minimal issues but long-term DPF and EGR reliability data still emerging EQ Power PHEV systems add battery management complexity - high-voltage battery degradation on early C350e is a known concern above 70,000 miles M178 in AMG C63 and E63 is a benchmark biturbo V8; hot-side turbo heat soak and oil coking in turbo feed pipes recorded on high-mileage track-used examples M264 1.5-litre unit in A-Class draws on Renault-partnership architecture - chain drive and oil consumption complaints on early production batches [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM654, M274, M178, OM642, M264, M256
years.cta: Find OM654 and M178 replacement engines for 2016–2018 Mercedes-Benz models

years.preview: EQ Electric Platform, OM654 Rollout Completes & Euro 6d 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Mercedes-Benz completed the brand-wide migration to the OM654 diesel family, launched the EQC as its first dedicated electric model in the UK, and brought Euro 6d emissions compliance across all drivetrains. Key Changes OM654 diesel fully deployed across A-Class, B-Class, CLA, GLB, GLE, GLS, and Sprinter EQC 400 4MATIC launched in UK - dual motor, 80 kWh battery, no combustion engine Euro 6d (RDE real-driving emissions) standard enforced - DPF + SCR mandatory on all diesel variants M256 3.0-litre inline-six turbo petrol with integrated 48V ISG replaces M276 V6 across E450 and S450 OM656 3.0-litre inline-six diesel with 48V MHEV introduced in GLE400d, GLS400d, and S400d Main Engines OM654 - 2.0 diesel (A220d, B220d, CLA220d, GLB220d, GLE300d) OM656 - 3.0 inline-six diesel MHEV (GLE400d, GLS400d, S400d) M256 - 3.0 inline-six turbo petrol + 48V ISG (E450, S450, GLE450) M177 - 4.0 biturbo V8 (AMG A45 S, AMG CLA45 S - 2.0 variant) EQC electric - dual motor (EQC 400 4MATIC) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz GLE-Class (W167) Mercedes-Benz A-Class (W177) Mercedes-Benz CLA (C118) Mercedes-Benz GLS-Class (X167) Mercedes-Benz EQC (N293) Known For OM656 inline-six diesel is one of the smoothest diesel units Mercedes-Benz has produced - MHEV integration reduces stop-start harshness considerably M256 inline-six petrol with ISG delivers seamless performance; ISG starter-generator unit failure is an emerging fault on examples above 50,000 miles Euro 6d compliance brings more sophisticated emissions hardware - NOx trap and GPF (gasoline particulate filter) on petrol models add new failure potential EQC battery thermal management is adequate but early adopters report faster-than-expected range degradation in cold UK winters [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM654, OM656, M256, M177, M264, EQC electric motors
years.cta: Compare 2019–2021 Mercedes-Benz replacement engines and MHEV units from UK specialists

years.preview: EQ Expansion, C-Class OM654 Refresh & AMG M139 Peak Power 🔴 Performance Landmark
years.description: Short intro: Mercedes-Benz pushed AMG four-cylinder output to world-record levels with the M139 2.0-litre unit, expanded the EQ all-electric range across six models, and delivered a refined second-generation OM654 in the all-new W206 C-Class. Key Changes OM654 Gen 2 introduced in W206 C-Class with revised combustion geometry and improved particulate filtering M139 2.0-litre turbo petrol AMG unit produces 421 bhp in A45 S - highest specific output of any production four-cylinder engine at launch EQS, EQE, EQB, and EQA expand the battery-electric lineup - no combustion engine in these models C-Class PHEV (C300e) standardises 25-mile electric range across the range Euro 6e standard begins phased introduction; WLTP-only homologation for new diesel derivatives Main Engines OM654 Gen 2 - 2.0 diesel (C220d W206, GLC220d X254, E220d W214) M139 - 2.0 turbo petrol AMG (A45 S, CLA45 S - 421 bhp) M256 - 3.0 inline-six petrol MHEV (E450, GLE450, GLS450) OM656 - 3.0 inline-six diesel MHEV (S400d, GLE400d, continued) EQ electric motors - single and dual motor (EQA, EQB, EQE, EQS) Popular Models Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W206) Mercedes-Benz GLC-Class (X254) Mercedes-Benz E-Class (W214) Mercedes-Benz A 45 S AMG Mercedes-Benz EQS / EQE Known For M139 sets a benchmark for four-cylinder performance; however, oil consumption above 8,000 rpm is noted on track-focused examples and turbo longevity under sustained high-load use is being monitored by specialists OM654 Gen 2 addresses some early-generation DPF shortcomings - real-world reliability data emerging positively but long-term chain and EGR data not yet fully established EQ platform battery warranties (8 years / 160,000 km from Mercedes-Benz) provide reasonable UK owner confidence; independent repair cost for battery pack replacement remains very high C300e PHEV represents the fastest-growing replacement enquiry segment on newer models - high-voltage battery and electric motor integration adds servicing complexity beyond traditional engine replacement [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: OM654 Gen 2, M139, M256, OM656, M177, EQS/EQE/EQA motor units Not sure which year your Mercedes-Benz was built? Enter your registration number and we'll identify the exact engine, model year, and compatible replacement options instantly - all backed by a minimum 12-month warranty from UK Mercedes-Benz specialists.

faq.h2: Mercedes-Benz Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Mercedes-Benz owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Mercedes-Benz engine typically costs between £2,800 and £5,200 for a reconditioned OM651 2.1 CDI (C220/E220/Sprinter) fitted by a UK specialist, while performance units like the OM642 3.0 V6 CDI or M177 4.0 V8 can range from £4,500 to £8,500+ supply and fit. Used engines start around £1,200–£2,500 for common codes but carry no warranty on internal wear. Main dealer replacement costs are often 40–60% higher than specialist pricing. Our advice: always compare three quotes, as prices for the same OM651 engine can vary by over £1,000 between suppliers. → Compare Mercedes-Benz engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Mercedes-Benz engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors determine your final cost: engine size and complexity (OM651 is cheaper than OM642 or M177), condition (reconditioned with upgraded parts costs more than used), vehicle age and model complexity (Sprinter and S‑Class labour times exceed C‑Class), and whether you choose supply‑only (£1,500–£3,000 for common diesels) or supply‑and‑fit (adding £800–£1,500 in labour). Location also matters — London and South‑East workshops charge roughly 20–30% more per hour than Midlands or Northern specialists. EngineMarket’s comparison tool shows you fitted prices upfront, so there are no hidden surprises. → Get quotes from UK Mercedes-Benz engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Mercedes-Benz?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if your Mercedes has a solid chassis, good gearbox, and the rest of the vehicle is in decent condition. A reconditioned OM651 engine with upgraded timing chain components costs £2,800–£4,200 fitted, which is significantly less than replacing a £6,000–£10,000 C‑Class or £8,000–£15,000 Sprinter. The alternative — scrapping the car and buying a used replacement — often costs more and leaves you with an unknown service history. Rebuilt engines frequently outlast the original because they incorporate design upgrades that address known failure points. On a high‑mileage C220 CDI or E350 CDI, replacement is almost always the financially sensible choice. → Find reconditioned Mercedes-Benz engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: What's included in a Mercedes-Benz engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit service includes sourcing the correct engine for your specific Mercedes model and engine code, removal of the failed unit, installation of the replacement (reconditioned or used), new oil, filters, coolant, and a full system flush. It also covers workmanship warranty (typically 12 months or longer), professional disposal of the old engine, and a post‑installation road test to verify correct operation. Fitting costs for a common model like the W204 C220 CDI typically add £800–£1,200 to the supply price. Specialist suppliers in the EngineMarket network provide transparent, all‑in quotes so you know exactly what’s included before you commit. → Compare Mercedes-Benz engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Mercedes-Benz engine replacement take?
items.answer: For a common Mercedes-Benz model like a C‑Class (W204/W205), E‑Class (W212/W213), or Sprinter with an OM651 or OM642 engine, a like‑for‑like replacement typically takes 3–5 working days. More complex models with advanced electronics — such as the S‑Class (W222), GLE, or AMG variants — may take 5–7 days due to additional systems requiring recalibration. The single biggest variable is engine availability: most suppliers hold stock of popular codes like OM651 and OM642, but rarer units (OM629, M157) may need sourcing, adding 2–5 days. Use our marketplace to find suppliers with your exact engine code in stock and ask about current lead times before ordering. → Get fast quotes for Mercedes-Benz engine replacement

items.question: Do reconditioned Mercedes-Benz engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every reconditioned and rebuilt Mercedes-Benz engine supplied through EngineMarket’s network carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Many specialist rebuilders offer extended 24‑month or 36‑month coverage on units that have undergone full bench testing and internal upgrades. Standard warranty covers major internal components: crankshaft, con‑rods, pistons, piston rings, bearings, timing chain assembly, oil pump, cylinder head, and valves. Wear items such as gaskets, seals, belts, and external ancillaries may have separate terms. Always confirm exactly what is and isn’t covered with your chosen supplier before work begins. → Compare Mercedes-Benz engine prices with warranty included

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Mercedes-Benz engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront (£1,200–£2,500 for common OM651 units) and work well as a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the car within 12 months. However, used engines carry the same original design weaknesses — timing chain stretch on OM651, oil cooler leaks on OM642, balance shaft wear on M272. Reconditioned engines cost more (£2,800–£5,200 fitted for common diesels) but include new pistons, rings, bearings, timing components, and upgraded parts that fix the original failure points. If you intend to keep your Mercedes for another 80,000+ miles, a reconditioned engine with a 12–24 month warranty is almost always the better long‑term investment. → Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Mercedes-Benz engine makes financial sense

items.question: What are the most common Mercedes-Benz engine failures?
items.answer: The most frequent failures driving engine replacement in the UK are: OM651 2.1 CDI timing chain stretch and oil pressure loss (C‑Class, E‑Class, Sprinter, Vito — 70k‑120k miles); OM642 3.0 V6 CDI oil cooler housing leaks destroying turbochargers (E350, ML350, GL350 — 80k‑130k miles); OM654 2.0 CDI AdBlue injector crystallisation and injector seizure in cylinder head (E220d, C220d — 40k‑80k miles for AdBlue, 80k‑120k for injectors); M272/M273 V6/V8 petrol balance shaft sprocket wear (2005‑2010 models — catastrophic timing failure); and M271 1.8 Kompressor camshaft adjuster failure (C180/C200 — 60k‑100k miles). Early detection rarely prevents these from becoming catastrophic, making replacement the most common outcome. → Get quotes for specialist Mercedes-Benz engine replacement

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Mercedes-Benz engine?
items.answer: Always compare at least three quotes — prices for the same OM651 or OM642 engine code can vary by £1,000+ between suppliers. If your budget is tight, a used engine with a short warranty (£1,200–£2,500) is the cheapest route, but for long‑term ownership, a reconditioned unit with upgraded components (£2,800–£5,200 fitted) delivers better value. Consider supply‑only if you have a trusted independent garage willing to fit it — this can save £300–£600 compared to a supplier’s fitting fee. Always ask whether delivery, fitting, VAT, and disposal of your old engine are included in the quoted price.
